coredata.dc:title: Tracing the geographical origin of Chinese green tea based on fluorescent sensor array combined with multi-way chemometrics analyses
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Xinyao
Yin, Chunling
Hu, Leqian
Zhao, Liuchuang
Chen, Mengyao
Hua, Xia
Liu, Zhimin
Li, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent sensor arrays are becoming a hot topic in many fields because they can simultaneously detect multiple targets in complex research systems. However, most researches have only collected two-dimensional fluorescence spectral data from fluorescent sensor array interacting with target analytes. In contrast, three-dimensional fluorescence spectra can provide richer information than two-dimensional fluorescence spectra. Based on the hypothesis that collecting three-dimensional fluorescence spectra can obtain more abundant information of green tea samples from different regions, which can improve the accuracy and reliability of origin identification. This study aimed to explore the feasibility of using three-dimensional fluorescent sensor array combined with multi-way pattern recognition methods for the origin discrimination of green tea based on the differences in the contents and types of metal ions in green tea. To investigate this, we first designed a fluorescent sensor array based on amino acid-derived carbon dots and examined its ability to recognize common metal ions in green tea. Excitation-emission matrix spectra of green tea extracts from different geographical origins after interaction with the fluorescent sensor array were collected. Several multi-way pattern recognition methods were used to analyze the three-dimensional fluorescent array data of 100 green tea samples from five origins. The overall classification results of green tea from the five geographical origins were satisfactory, with the best prediction accuracy reaching 96.88%. In comparison, multilinear partial least squares discriminant analysis could make full use of the information of three-dimensional fluorescence data. And its correct identification results for green tea were superior to those of unfold partial least squares discriminant analysis. These results sufficiently demonstrated that the fluorescent sensor array integrated with multi-way pattern recognition, has promising potential for tracing the origin of Chinese green tea.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent sensor array
Green tea
Excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectra
Multi-way pattern recognition methods
Geographical origin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of green tea and its extracts
Responses of fluorescent array sensor to the 15 metal ions
EEM spectra of five different origins of green tea
Origin traceability of green tea samples
Identification of the green tea samples by N‑PLS‑DA
Identification of the green tea samples by U‑PLS‑DA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Geographical distribution of green tea samples. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence spectra at excitation wavelengths from 290 nm to 410 nm of HisCDs, ArgCDs, SerCDs (10 μg·mL−1).
Fingerprint of the fluorescent sensor array toward 15 metal ions.

The EEM spectra of fluorescent sensor array interacting with green tea samples (Including five origins of green tea samples and three carbon dots). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The M-PCA score plots of the green tea samples based on preprocessed EEMs data. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The estimated dummy vectors by N-PLS-DA and U-PLS-DA refer to the variety memberships of the green tea samples in the training sets and the prediction sets. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Sensitivity, specificity, precision and accuracy corresponding to the five different origins of green tea samples obtained from the confusion matrixes of N-PLS-DA and U-PLS-DA.

head.ce:title: Tracing the geographical origin of Chinese green tea based on fluorescent sensor array combined with multi-way chemometrics analyses

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Complexity Science, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou 450001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Complexity Science, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent sensor arrays are becoming a hot topic in many fields because they can simultaneously detect multiple targets in complex research systems. However, most researches have only collected two-dimensional fluorescence spectral data from fluorescent sensor array interacting with target analytes. In contrast, three-dimensional fluorescence spectra can provide richer information than two-dimensional fluorescence spectra. Based on the hypothesis that collecting three-dimensional fluorescence spectra can obtain more abundant information of green tea samples from different regions, which can improve the accuracy and reliability of origin identification. This study aimed to explore the feasibility of using three-dimensional fluorescent sensor array combined with multi-way pattern recognition methods for the origin discrimination of green tea based on the differences in the contents and types of metal ions in green tea. To investigate this, we first designed a fluorescent sensor array based on amino acid-derived carbon dots and examined its ability to recognize common metal ions in green tea. Excitation-emission matrix spectra of green tea extracts from different geographical origins after interaction with the fluorescent sensor array were collected. Several multi-way pattern recognition methods were used to analyze the three-dimensional fluorescent array data of 100 green tea samples from five origins. The overall classification results of green tea from the five geographical origins were satisfactory, with the best prediction accuracy reaching 96.88%. In comparison, multilinear partial least squares discriminant analysis could make full use of the information of three-dimensional fluorescence data. And its correct identification results for green tea were superior to those of unfold partial least squares discriminant analysis. These results sufficiently demonstrated that the fluorescent sensor array integrated with multi-way pattern recognition, has promising potential for tracing the origin of Chinese green tea.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: A traditional chemical sensor is defined as a device that selectively responds to a specific analyte through physical or chemical interactions, which can be used for qualitative or quantitative determination of the analyte (Collins et al., 2007). Fluorescence sensor belongs to a kind of optical sensor in chemical sensor, also known as fluorescence probe. Fluorescent probes have been widely used in the qualitative and quantitative analysis of metal ions because of their high selectivity and sensitivity (Qiao et al., 2021). Fluorescence sensor usually requires designing a highly selective and specific fluorescent probe for the detection of the target metal ion. This technique is very suitable for the identification of a specific target molecule in a specific context (Sun et al., 2020). However, this method will not be suitable for simultaneous analysis of multiple components. It would be time consuming, cumbersome and impractical to simultaneously design highly selective fluorescent probes for multiple detection components. (Wu et al., 2017b). Therefore, researchers have developed sensor array, which can efficiently solve these problems. Sensor arrays, differing from traditional chemical sensors with strict “lock-and-key” design principles, consist of multiple non-selective or weakly selective sensing units (Liu et al., 2022). Sensor array is capable of generating a unique response for each analyte. Each response can be captured by the detector and convert into one or more measurable signals, such as fluorescence intensity, wavelength, or colorimetric signals, which can obtain a unique “finger-print” for pattern recognition (Yan et al., 2022). According to the difference of detection signal type, sensor array can be divided into colorimetric sensor array, fluorescent sensor array, and electrochemical sensor array (Chen et al., 2024b). Fluorescent sensor array has the advantages of high sensitivity, fast analysis speed, and rich output signals, which plays an important role in optical sensor array. Currently, sensor array has been widely used in many fields such as environmental monitoring (Zhou et al., 2021), chemical sensing (Lin et al., 2024), and biomedical diagnostics (Wu et al., 2017a). In these fields, sensor array is mostly used for qualitative and quantitative analysis of metal ions, biothiols, antibiotics or proteins (Long et al., 2024).

Tea is one of the most popular beverages all over the world. It is rich in proteins, tea polyphenols, vitamins, amino acids, as well as essential trace elements such as iron, copper, zinc and manganese, which have functional characteristics against serious disease, such as cancer, obesity, and cardiovascular disease (Rana et al., 2021). There are various types of tea, such as green tea, black tea, white tea, etc. Green tea, as the second most traditional tea in the world, accounts for 20 %–22 % of global tea consumption (Chu et al., 2023). Due to the difference in the variety of green tea, the growing environment, harvesting time, the chemical composition of green tea changes accordingly, resulting in the quality and taste of green tea are very different (Hu et al., 2022b). Generally, the higher the quality of green tea, the higher the price. This phenomenon may lead to fraud. Unscrupulous traders may fabricate the product labels of ordinary products, make false claims about the geographical origin of green tea, or mix inferior or ordinary varieties of green tea with high-quality green tea to deceive consumers and make huge profits (Shuai et al., 2022). These phenomena seriously disrupt the normal market operations, and lead consumers to lose faith in the quality of green tea. Therefore, reliable methods are used to limit or eliminate these risks of counterfeiting to ensure the compliance of green tea (Miyauchi et al., 2017).

In general, tea quality is evaluated by professional tea evaluators, which requires a significant amount of time and labor. The evaluation process is susceptible to environmental factors such as lighting, temperature, and humidity, as well as the subjective experiences, emotions, and physiological conditions of the expert panels (Zhu et al., 2017). Therefore, the tea industry is looking for an objective evaluation system to assess the variety and quality of green tea. In recent years, a series of instrumental analytical techniques have been applied to protect the authenticity of green tea, but the research still has some limitations. Among them, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) and its combination with inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) (Habte et al., 2016) have been employed because of their high sensitive, low detection limits and the possibility of simultaneous multiple elements analyses. However, the method requires expensive equipment, and has strict environmental requirements, which is difficult to acquire the requirements of widespread use. Chromatography and its relative hyphenated techniques are also used to discriminate the geographical origins of green tea due to its high separation efficiency and low sample size, but the methods require professional operators and considerable time for testing (Yun et al., 2021; Jin et al., 2023; Peng et al., 2023). Many Optical methods have been applied in the origin tracing of green tea. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Esteki et al., 2022) can provide abundant chemical structural information, which is helpful in identifying organic components in tea. Near-infrared spectroscopy (NIR) (Jin et al., 2022) has simple preprocessing and can achieve non-destructive analysis. However, both FT-IR and NIR require the sample to be homogeneous, and they perform poorly in detecting subtle chemical component differences (Shen et al., 2024). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy (Palacios-Morillo et al., 2013) is often used for qualitative analysis due to its simplicity and speed. In comparison with other optical methods such as FT-IR, UV–Vis spectroscopy provides relatively limited chemical information. Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) (Chen et al., 2024c) has the advantages of high sensitivity and fast analysis speed, but the sample pre-treatment requirements are strict and requires professional operators. Among these optical methods, fluorescence spectroscopy is widely used due to its high sensitivity, abundant fluorescence signals, convenient operation and low cost (Fan et al., 2022).

Fluorescent sensor array is a fast and efficient technique for chemical analysis owing to its high sensitivity, low detection limits, simplicity and good repeatability (Li et al., 2024a). Currently, fluorescent sensor array has been used in the fields of food security. Xu et al. constructed a novel fluorescent sensor array based on glutathione-protected gold nanoclusters probes, which successfully classified and quantified nine organic acids. Moreover, this study successfully recognized 21 Baijiu samples of different brands. It could also distinguish Baijiu of different qualities as well as pure and adulterated Baijiu (Xu et al., 2023). However, most studies have collected two-dimensional fluorescence spectral data from fluorescent sensor array interacting with target analytes and combined it with pattern recognition methods to distinguish the target analytes. The excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectra (EEMs), also known as three-dimensional fluorescence, is a three-dimensional fingerprint consisting of the excitation wavelength, emission wavelength, and fluorescence intensity. Compared with the two-dimensional fluorescence method, the three-dimensional fingerprint is unique for each measured sample and provides more detailed and comprehensive information about fluorescent species present in analyzed samples (Chen et al., 2024a), which has a wide range of applications in the field of food chemical analysis.

To date, no studies have collected three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of fluorescent sensor array interacting with green tea samples to distinguish green tea from different origins. The aim of this study was to explore the feasibility of using three-dimensional fluorescent sensor array combined with multi-way pattern recognition methods for the origin discrimination of green tea based on the differences in the species and contents of metal ions in green tea from different origins.

All reagents were used as received and with no further purification. L-histidine, L-arginine, L-serine, anhydrous citric acid, Co(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3, and Cr(NO3)3 were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaNO3, KNO3, Ca(NO3)2, Mg(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3, Al(NO3)3, Cd(NO3)2, disodium hydrogen phosphate, sodium hydroxide, nitric acid and 30 % hydrogen peroxide were bought from Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Hg(NO3)2 was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China).
In this study, the three carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal method in reference. According to the amino acid, the resultant carbon dots were abbreviated as HisCDs, ArgCDs and SerCDs.

Briefly, to prepare HisCDs (Mohagheghpour et al., 2022), anhydrous citric acid (0.5 mM) and L-histidine (1 mM) were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water and ultrasonicated for 5 min to dissolve them into a clear solution. Then the solution was sealed into a 50 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 180 ℃ for 6 h. After that, the reaction was naturally cooled to room temperature. The products, which is dark yellow-black was dialyzed against ultrapure water with a membrane bag (MWCO = 1000) for 24 h to remove the excess precursors.

To prepare ArgCDs (Zhang et al., 2017), anhydrous citric acid (0.5 mM) and L-arginine (1 mM) were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water and ultrasonicated for 5 min to dissolve them into a clear solution. Then the solution was sealed into a 50 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 180 ℃ for 6 h. After that, the reaction was naturally cooled to room temperature. The products, which is orange-red was dialyzed against ultrapure water with a membrane bag (MWCO = 1000) for 24 h to remove the excess precursors.

To prepare SerCDs (Wang et al., 2017), anhydrous citric acid (0.5 mM) and L-serine (1 mM) were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water and ultrasonicated for 5 min to dissolve them into a clear solution. Then the solution was sealed into a 50 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 180 ℃ for 6 h. After the reaction was naturally cooled to room temperature, it was filtered with 0.22 μm microporous filter membranes to remove insoluble impurities, and then the black-brown product was dialyzed against ultrapure water with a membrane bag (MWCO = 1000) for 24 h to remove the excess precursors. The above purified samples were collected separately and freeze-dried to obtain solid products for further applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of green tea and its extracts
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, 100 samples of five categories of green tea from different geographical origins in China were purchased from a local green tea supermarket, with 20 batches of samples from each origin. All green tea samples purchased in this study were authenticated by Professor Xiaolong Xie at the School of Pharmacy, Henan University of Chinese Medicine, China. The origin and characteristics of green tea are shown in Fig. 1 and Table S1, respectively.

The green tea samples from five geographic origins were dried at 60 °C for 2 h, crushed using at FW-80 high-speed smashing machine (Beijing, China), sieved through 120 mesh, and stored in a dryer for future use. Next, accurately weigh 0.5000 g of green tea powder into a 50 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave, added 8 mL of concentrated nitric acid and 2 mL of 30 % hydrogen peroxide. The above solution was first left overnight for cold digestion, and put into the oven at 120 ℃ for 2 h, and then heated up to 160 ℃ for 6.0 h. After that, the reaction was cooled naturally to obtain the green tea extracts, which was filtered by 0.45 μm microporous filter membranes, adjusted the pH to 6 with NaOH, transferred to a 50 mL volumetric flask, and then fixed with ultrapure water to the scale.

The fluorescence spectra of the fluorescent sensor array interacting with different metal ions were collected at room temperature and dry environment by using the F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) with a 150 W xenon lamp. All measurements were performed in a 1.0 cm quartz cell. Excitation wavelength was the optimal excitation wavelength for each of the three carbon dots. The emission wavelength range was 300–650 nm. The slit width was 5.0 nm. The scanning speed was 1200 nm/min. Three kinds of carbon dots solution at concentration of 10 μg·mL−1 and 1
×
10−3 mol·L−1 metal ion solution (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cr3+) were prepared. Added 1.0 mL of each metal ion solution to 3.0 mL of HisCDs solution and fixed to 10 mL with disodium hydrogen phosphate-citric acid buffer solution (pH = 6.0). After incubating for approximately 30 min at room temperature, the fluorescence of the mixed solution was measured, and the corresponding intensity was recorded as I. The fluorescence intensity of the blank sample without the addition of metal ions was recorded as I0. The ratio of (I0-I)/I0 represented the fluorescence response of HisCDs against different metal ions. Each measurement was replicated three times. The same procedure was used for ArgCDs and SerCDs.

EEM fluorescence spectra were measured in the excitation wavelength range of 240–520 nm with 2 nm intervals and in the emission wavelength range of 300–610 nm with an interval of 2 nm, which gave 141 and 156 data points along the excitation and emission wavelength axes respectively. The slit width was 5.0 nm and the scanning speed was 12000 nm/min.

Based on the above mentioned parameters, add 3 mL of HisCDs (10 μg·mL−1) solution and 1.0 mL of green tea sample extract to a 10 mL brown volumetric flask, and the solution was fixed with disodium hydrogen phosphate-citric acid buffer solution (pH = 6.0). After incubation for 30 min, the fluorescence response of HisCDs was recorded. The same procedure was used for ArgCDs and SerCDs. Green tea from five different geographical origins was reacted with three carbon dots respectively, and 20 batches of samples were collected from each origin. Thus, a data matrix of 3 sensing elements
×
5 origins
×
20 samples can be obtained for further analysis.

The EEMs would provide more comprehensive information about samples. Each fluorescent EEM measurement acquires an excitation-emission matrix data (I × J) for one sample, where J and K represent the number of excitation and emission channels, respectively. I samples can be organized into an I × J × K three-way data array X. This three-way data array X has an internally mathematical structure called trilinearity (Peng et al., 2019). Therefore, the traditional two-dimensional pattern recognition methods such as principal component analysis (PCA), linear discriminant analysis (LDA), and partial least squares analysis (PLS) are no longer suitable for the classification of the EEMs data. A multi-way pattern recognition method would be applied to analyze the EEMs data. In this study, three kinds of chemometric multi-way pattern recognition methods including multi-way principal component analysis (M-PCA), multilinear partial least squares discriminant analysis (N-PLS-DA) and unfold partial least squares discriminant analysis (U-PLS-DA) were used to establish the green tea origin traceability model. The MATLAB (MathWorks, Natick, MA, USA) was used for EEM spectral data processing.

Principal component analysis (PCA) is an unsupervised classification method, similar to clustering, that reduces the data dimensionality to facilitate the visualization of the data (Lever et al., 2017). Multi-way principal component analysis (M-PCA) is an extension of PCA for three-dimensional data.(Pardo et al., 2013). For three-dimensional fluorescence spectroscopy, each fluorescent EEM measurement acquires the excitation-emission matrix (EEM) data (J × K) for one sample, where J and K represent the number of excitation and emission channels, respectively. Therefore, I samples can be organized into an I × J × K three-way data array X. M-PCA will first unfolds the matrix X into a new matrix X = I × JK, and then the principal component analysis will be performed to decompose X into the product of the score vector tr and the load vector
p r
, plus the residual matrix E.
(1)
X _
=
∑
i = 1
R
t r
⊗
p r
+ E
where R is the principal component fraction, ⊗ is the Kronecker product of the matrix, and the rest is consistent with the principal component analysis resolution process.

Partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) is a supervised linear classification method, which ingeniously combines the characteristics of PLS regression with the discrimination ability of LDA (Zhang et al., 2022). Multilinear partial least squares discriminant analysis (N-PLS-DA) is the result of an extension of PLS-DA, which extends the traditional PLS to higher orders and directly uses the multi-way data structures for model building and prediction. The three-dimensional data matrix group X (I × J × K) and the sample category matrix group Y (I × M × N) are decomposed separately.(Wang et al., 2023).
(2)
X _
= T
G X
W
K
⊗
W
J
T
+
E _
X _
(3)
Y _
= U
G Y
Q
N
⊗
Q
M
T
+
E _
Y _
T and U are score matrices, WK, WJ and QN, QM are weight matrices, GX and GY are hidden variable matrices, and
E _
X _
and
E _
Y _
are error matrices, and the matrix decomposition establishes a functional relationship between the T and U score matrices, with the chemical significance being that there is a functional relationship between the sample measurement data and the sample category, which can be subsequently used to train or predict the sample category.

Unfold partial least squares discriminant analysis (U-PLS-DA) is a variation of the PLS-DA. Unlike N-PLS-DA, U-PLS-DA does not use a trilinear model to decompose the three-way data array, it first unfolds the three-way data array along the row of the matrix X (J × K) into a row vector (1×(J × K)). Therefore, if there are I samples, it can obtain a matrix with the size of (I×(J × K)), and then the PLS-DA algorithm will be used for analysis and classification (Long et al., 2021). In this study, the only difference between U-PLS-DA and N-PLS-DA is that N-PLS-DA is directly used for EEMs data, whereas U-PLS-DA is used to unfold EEMs datasets. The unfolding strategy has the benefit of fully using all of the information in the second-order data and allowing the application of classification techniques based on two-way data. However, once unfolded, a very large data matrix is created, and the number of irrelevant variables may far exceed the number of actually meaningful variables, resulting in complex and time-consuming calculations (Yuan et al., 2020).

Four parameters of sensitivity, specificity, precision and accuracy were used to evaluate the performance of N-PLS-DA and U-PLS-DA models in this study (Wu et al., 2024). Sensitivity is an important evaluation metric that measures the ability of a model to correctly predict all actual positive samples. Specificity refers to the ratio of the number of samples that the model correctly identifies as negative class to the total number of negative class samples. Precision indicates the proportion of truly positive class samples among the samples that the model identifies as positive class. Accuracy is the most commonly used classification performance metric (Li et al., 2024b). It can be used to indicate the accuracy of a model and refers to the ratio of the number of samples correctly predicted in all experiments to the number of all samples. In general, the higher the accuracy of the model, the better the model is. The formulas for sensitivity, specificity, precision, and accuracy are as follows, where TP is the true positive, FP is the false positive, TN is the true negative and FN is the false negative.
(4)
Sensitivity = T P /
T P + F N
(5)
Specificity = T N /
T N + F P
(6)
Precision = T P /
T P + F P
(7)
Accuracy =
T P + T N
/
T N + T P + F N + F P

The structure of carbon dots was characterized using FT-IR and XRD. The results showed that HisCDs, ArgCDs, and SerCDs exhibit similar characteristics, but there are some differences. In FT-IR analysis of three carbon dots are shown in Fig. S1, which shows that the carbon dots are rich in surface groups. The stretching vibration peaks of O–H/N–H (3490 cm−1), C=O (1700 cm−1) indicate that the carbon dots include amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. Furthermore, the absorption peaks at 2700–3000 cm−1, 1576 cm−1, 1390 cm−1 and 1178 cm−1 correspond to υ(C–H), υ(N–H), δ(C–H) and υ(C–N), indicating that the carbon dots contain nitrogenous groups and aromatic structures (Hu et al., 2022a). As can be seen from Fig. S2, a wide diffraction peak at 23° is seen in three carbon dots, indicating the presence of an amorphous carbon phase.

To elucidate the unique optical properties of carbon dots, we performed UV–Vis and fluorescence characterization, as shown in Fig. S3 and Fig. 2 . HisCDs and SerCDs showed the the same absorption peaks near 220 nm and 296 nm with different intensity. The absorption peaks of ArgCDs were located at approximately 210 nm and 334 nm, respectively, which could be attributed to the π-π* electron transition of the conjugated C=C and the n-π* electron transition of the C=O (Ding et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2019). All the carbon dots showed a classic excitation-dependent emission behavior. Specifically, the excitation wavelength moves from 290 nm to 410 nm, the fluorescence peaks undergo gradual wavelength shifts. However, the maximum emission wavelengths were not the same, which were correlated with their surface defects and the sizes of the carbon dots (Qian et al., 2014). HisCDs obtained the optimal fluorescence emission at a wavelength of 407 nm when the excitation wavelength was 320 nm. The maximum fluorescence intensity of ArgCDs was seen at 419 nm under 330 nm excitation. The SerCDs achieved the optimal fluorescence emission at a wavelength location of 445 nm when the excitation wavelength was 370 nm. The structure and optical properties of the carbon dots are consistent with the reference, indicating that three carbon dots have been successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Responses of fluorescent array sensor to the 15 metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of the reaction solution and the incubation time of metal ions were optimized before testing. As shown in Fig. S4 and Fig. S5, pH 6.0 was the optimal recognition performance for the fluorescent sensor array. The fluorescence response reached balance after incubation for thirty minutes.

In order to test the identification ability of the fluorescent sensor array, the response fingerprints of 15 metal ions (100 μM) on the fluorescent sensor array were obtained at optimal excitation wavelengths of three carbon dots. The signal change value was defined as (I0-I)/I0, where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensity of the CDs in the absence and presence of the target metal ion, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence changes of the fluorescent array sensor mixed with 15 metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cr3+) were different. It was observed that Hg2+ and Fe3+ almost quenched the original fluorescence of three carbon dots. For HisCDs, the fluorescence intensity was only slightly enhanced by Ca2+, Mn2+ and Cd2+. Mg2+, Co2+, Mn2+ and Cd2+ slightly enhanced the fluorescence intensity of ArgCDs, whereas all the other metal ions led to the Fluorescence quenching of ArgCDs. In addition to Hg2+ and Fe3+, all metal ions resulted in a significant decrease in SerCDs fluorescence intensity. In summary, for the same carbon dots, different metal ions have different effects on the fluorescence intensity. On the other hand, for the same metal ion, the value of fluorescence intensity also depends on different carbon dots. The results could be explained with the difference in chelate formation capability of metal ions with the functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots from different amino acid derivatives, such as amino, carboxyl, hydroxyl etc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EEM spectra of five different origins of green tea
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows the representative EEM spectra of green tea extracts from five different geographical origins combined with three carbon dots respectively, and the EEM spectra of three blank carbon dots. A total of 18 EEM spectral profiles are displayed. In the excitation spectrum range of 248–425 nm and the emission spectrum range of 350–550 nm, HisCDs and ArgCDs displayed two characteristic peaks that were similar in excitation and emission wavelength but varied in intensity. Compared to HisCDs and ArgCDs, SerCDs had different excitation and emission wavelengths and intensities of the two characteristic peaks. Though green tea is a multifluorophoric system (including tea polyphenols and amino acids, etc.), the green tea samples in this study were almost non-fluorescent. This may be due to the destruction of endogenous fluorescent substances during the extraction of metal ions from green tea by the wet acid digestion.

It can be seen from Fig. 4 that after adding carbon dots to green tea samples from different origin, there is no significant change in the region of characteristic peaks. However, the fluorescence intensity of the characteristic peaks was significantly reduced, especially in the excitation spectrum range of 248–290 nm and the emission spectrum range of 300–550 nm. This indicates that the carbon dots have interacted with the metal ions in green tea samples from different origins, leading to the decrease in fluorescence intensity. Because the fingerprints of green tea samples from different origins were almost the same and difficult to distinguish with visual evaluation of the differences between EEM spectra, the multi-way pattern recognition methods would be used to analyze the three-dimensional fluorescence data and further differentiate the origins of green tea.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Origin traceability of green tea samples

ce:section.ce:para: M-PCA is an unsupervised pattern recognition technique that was applied as the first step to detect the clustering possibility of green tea EEMs data array. Standard normal variable transformation was conducted on the spectral data from 100 sets of green tea samples before applying the M-PCA analysis. The M-PCA model employed the three principal components (PC1, PC2, and PC3) as the projection axes, transforming the original data on the new coordinate axes to derive the M-PCA score map, which is visualized for intuitive interpretation. It was found that the variance represented by PC1, PC2 and PC3 was 83.73 %, 6.47 % and 2.74 % respectively. The cumulative percent variance of the first three principal variables was found to be 92.94 % of the total variance in the whole EEMs datasets. It can be considered that the first three principal components represent most of the original data information. As can be seen from Fig. 5 , M-PCA showed that the five different green tea samples had a clustering trend. Both Henan green tea and Zhejiang green tea could be effectively separated from green tea of other origins. However, Anhui green tea, Hubei green tea and Fujian green tea were clustered together and difficult to distinguish. Previous studies have revealed that the types and contents of metal ions in green tea from different regions are different. Table S2 shows the contents of metal ions in green tea from different origins. It can be observed that the contents of Na+ in green tea from Anhui, Fujian, and Hubei were similar and much higher than that in green tea from Henan and Zhejiang. Compared to Zhejiang green tea, the contents of Mg2+, Hg2+, and Cr3+ in Henan green tea is lower. The contents of Pb2+, Fe3+ and Ni2+ in green tea from Anhui and Fujian were similar, and the contents of Cr3+ and Cu2+ from Anhui and Hubei did not differ much. Fig. S6 demonstrates the correlation between geographical factors (longitude, latitude, and altitude) and metal ions in green tea. It can be found that there are different degrees of correlation between metal ions and the three geographical factors. Cu2+ and Cr3+ have strong correlations with latitude, Mg2+ and Hg2+ are highly correlated with longitude. Na+, Pb2+, Fe3+ and Ni2+ have strong correlations with altitude. The results indicated that longitude, latitude and altitude might affect the accumulation of metal ions in green tea. Yichang in Hubei Province is on the same latitude line as Huangshan in Anhui Province, and Huangshan in Anhui Province is on the same longitude line as Quanzhou in Fujian Province. The similar geographic environment may lead to similar types and contents of metal ions in green tea, so that Anhui green tea, Hubei green tea and Fujian green tea were clustered together and difficult to distinguish. Overall, M-PCA can provide the overall clustering trend of different green tea samples, but the classification results are not ideal. In order to better obtain the results of origin traceability of green tea samples, the supervised multi-way pattern recognition methods N-PLS-DA and U-PLS-DA were used to classify green tea of different geographical origins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of the green tea samples by N‑PLS‑DA
ce:section.ce:para: N-PLS-DA is a strictly multi-way classification algorithm. It can make the utmost use of full fluorescence spectral information to obtain more accurate and reliable classification results. The green tea samples were divided into 5 groups, Anhui, Fujian, Hubei, Henan and Zhejiang, based on their province of origin. In this study, binary dummy codes were used to encode each green tea sample from different origins. Green tea samples from the 5 different origins were coded with 1 or 0. Green tea samples belonging to the category were labelled as 1 and those not belonging to the category were labelled as 0. The details were as follows: Anhui green tea (1, 0, 0, 0, 0), Fujian green tea (0, 1, 0, 0, 0), Hubei green tea (0, 0, 1, 0, 0), Henan green tea (0, 0, 0, 1, 0), Zhejiang green tea (0, 0, 0, 0, 1). All the green tea samples from different geographical origins were separated into training sets and prediction sets, using a random sampling algorithm. About two-thirds of the samples were randomly selected for the training set, and about one-third were randomly selected for the prediction set. Among them, 68 samples were used to construct training set and the remaining 32 samples constituted the prediction set. Fig. 6 displays the estimated dummy vectors by N-PLS-DA for the training and prediction set. The recognition of green tea samples was determined by locating the maximal dummy code value of the samples. The ultimate classification result of the N-PLS-DA model is determined by identifying the column where the maximum value of the estimated virtual vector is located. As shown in Fig. 6, in the first row of the dummy matrix, the first sample has the maximum dummy code value, which means that the first sample is categorized as Anhui green tea. In the same way, the classification results of the remaining samples were acquired according to the estimated dummy codes.

The classification results provided by N-PLS-DA are displayed in Table 1 . It could be seen that the overall accurate recognition rates for both the training and prediction sets were particularly excellent. In the training and prediction sets of the N-PLS-DA model, all five kinds of origin green teas can be accurately identified with the recognition rates of 100 % and 96.88 % respectively. In the prediction sets, only one Fujian green tea was incorrectly predicted and misjudged.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of the green tea samples by U‑PLS‑DA
ce:section.ce:para:
For comparison, U-PLS-DA was also used discriminate the geographic origins of green tea samples in this work. U-PLS-DA also can use the full fluorescence spectral information to discriminate the origin of green tea. Unlike N-PLS-DA, U-PLS-DA does not use the trilinear model to decompose the three-way data arrays, it first unfolds the three-dimensional fluorescence data matrix 100
×
141
×
156 (samples
×
number of λex
×
number of λem) along the spectral direction to form the new matrix data 100 × 21996, which is then analyzed and classified using the PLS-DA algorithm. Fig. 6 shows the estimated dummy vectors by U-PLS-DA for the training and prediction sets. As with the N-PLS-DA model, we can obtain the final discernment result of training sets and predicted sets. The classification results of the U-PLS-DA model are shown in Table 1. The recognition rates of the U-PLS-DA model for the training and prediction sets are 100 % and 87.50 %, respectively. In the prediction sets, one Hubei green tea was incorrectly predicted as Fujian green tea, and three Henan green teas were incorrectly predicted as Hubei green tea.

The classification ability of N-PLS-DA and U-PLS-DA was assessed by the classical parameters sensitivity, specificity, precision and accuracy of training and prediction sets. N-PLS-DA model and U-PLS-DA model made full use of all the fluorescence information of the green tea samples. As could be seen from the values in Table 1, the figure of merit showed that the N-PLS-DA model was obviously better than the U-PLS-DA model. When processing data using U-PLS-DA, the three-dimensional fluorescence data matrix (number of samples
×
number of λex
×
number of λem) is first unfolded along the spectral direction to form a new two-dimensional matrix, a process that may destroy the three-linear structure of the data (Salvatore et al., 2013). Additionally, the data becomes very large after unfolding, which reduces the computational efficiency and may also lead to more complex models with poorer predictive power.

Based on the different types and contents of metal ions in green tea from different places, a fluorescent sensor array prepared by amino acid derivative carbon dots was constructed, and successfully applied to the origin tracing of green tea. We first collected three-dimensional fluorescence fingerprint spectra of green tea extracts combined with fluorescent sensor array in this study. Three multi-way pattern recognition methods, M-PCA, N-PLS-DA and U-PLS-DA, were used to analyze the fluorescent array spectral data of the five origin areas respectively. The unsupervised pattern recognition algorithm M-PCA demonstrated the feasibility of green tea origin tracing based on fluorescence data of green tea extracts from different origins interacting with three probe arrays. Supervised pattern recognition methods N-PLS-DA and U-PLS-DA give more satisfactory classification results. In comparison, N-PLS-DA model is superior to U-PLS-DA model, and the classification accuracy of its training set is 100 %, and the classification accuracy of its prediction set is 96.88 %.

In comparison with previous methods for green tea origin traceability, there are two main advantages of the method used in this study. The first advantage is that the fluorescence method is more sensitive, convenient and simpler. The second advantage is that this method primarily collected three-dimensional fluorescence fingerprint spectra of green tea extracts interacting with fluorescent sensor array, instead of collecting two-dimensional fluorescence spectra. Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra can provide more comprehensive information than two-dimensional fluorescence spectra. The three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum can be regarded as a unique fingerprint for each sample, which makes the classification results more accurate and reliable. All in all, the fluorescent sensor array provides a reliable method for the origin traceability of green tea, which is conducive to maintaining the market order of green tea and and also provides a new idea for food authenticity identification.

Xinyao Lin: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Chunling Yin: Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Leqian Hu: Writing – review & editing, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Liuchuang Zhao: Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Mengyao Chen: Investigation, Data curation. Xia Hua: Software, Data curation. Zhimin Liu: Validation, Conceptualization. Peng Li: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the the support from the National Natural Science Foundation China (No. 22305068) and the Research fund for high-level talents of Henan University of Technology (No. 31490142).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodres.2025.115838.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
